metadata.analysis_title: AXIA Training Data vs. Official Benchmark Use Case Comparison

section_A_training_gaps_vs_axia.summary: 18 named training topics found in training data. Of these, 7 have no matching AXIA benchmark example, and 11 have only partial matches.

absent_from_axia.gap_analysis: No AXIA benchmark example specifically covers employee benefits/tax form exfiltration. This is a significant training data category (729 samples) with no AXIA benchmark equivalent.
No AXIA benchmark example covers health/insurance claim data. Training data has 225 samples in critical tier. Possible gap - AXIA may not have benchmarked insurance claim scenarios.
No AXIA benchmark example covers daily market commentary. Training data has 431 samples across high/medium/low sensitivity. Likely low-risk content that AXIA did not need to benchmark.
No AXIA benchmark example for system maintenance notifications. Training data has 234 samples (medium/low). Expected - these are generally benign and do not represent IRM scenarios.
No AXIA benchmark example for compliance policy updates. 108 training samples (medium). Not a typical IRM scenario.
No AXIA benchmark example for quarterly business reviews. 115 training samples (medium). Could partially relate to ALCO or board materials scenarios but no direct match.
No AXIA benchmark example for IT security patch notes. 115 training samples (medium). Not a typical IRM scenario - this is IT ops content.

partial_matches_only.training_topic: Wire Transfer or Payment with Account Details
partial_matches_only.gap_analysis: Training data covers wire transfer data as a document type (structural/PII focus). AXIA example 5 is about MODIFICATION of wire instructions for fraud - a behavioral/fraud scenario not represented in training data. Training data treats this as a sensitivity classification exercise, not as a behavioral fraud pattern.

partial_matches_only.training_topic: Customer Account Opening with Identity Documents
partial_matches_only.gap_analysis: Training data covers KYC/onboarding documents with PII. Partial match to AML/KYC examples. But training data does not capture the INTENT dimension - whether KYC docs are being exported maliciously vs. processed legitimately.

Training data has payroll records as a document type. Partially matches AXIA payroll oversharing scenarios. But AXIA examples have specific behavioral contexts (wrong recipient, policy bypass) not reflected in training data topics.

partial_matches_only.training_topic: Loan or Credit Application with Personal Information
partial_matches_only.gap_analysis: Training data covers loan/credit applications. Partially matches AXIA examples involving credit risk data or mortgage data. Training data is generic PII sensitivity; AXIA examples focus on specific exfiltration scenarios.

partial_matches_only.training_topic: Funding Round and Cap Table Details
partial_matches_only.gap_analysis: Training data covers funding/cap table docs. Partially maps to AXIA M&A and insider trading examples. But AXIA specificity (pre-board approval timing, specific recipients) is absent in training data.
Training data has client statements with portfolio data. Partial match to AXIA wealth management scenarios. Missing the UHNW context and departing employee behavioral signal.
Training data has internal audit reports. Maps to AXIA audit finding exposure examples. But training data lacks the recipient context (media vs. board vs. personal email).

partial_matches_only.training_topic: Customer Contact List with Personal Details
partial_matches_only.gap_analysis: Training data covers customer contact lists. Maps to CRM/contact data scenarios in AXIA. Missing the departing employee and bulk export behavioral context of example 14.
Training data has portfolio/risk metrics. Weak match to ALCO materials scenario. AXIA example 48 is about unauthorized BCC to academic - behavioral context absent in training data.

Training data covers vendor contracts. Maps to AXIA vendor contract sharing examples. Training data is document-type focused; AXIA examples include the recipient relationship (competing vendor, board candidate).

Training data has customer complaints with account details. Loose match to AXIA example 39 (internal forwarding). Training data treats this as document sensitivity; AXIA example is about authorized internal routing.

behavioral_signals_absent.description: Training data is document-type/PII focused. The 'unknown' category (65.1% of all samples, 27,953 records from Enron emails and Slack) covers operational communications but NOT as IRM-specific behavioral scenarios.
key_missing_behavioral_signals: Destination analysis (personal email, dark web, competitor domain, social media)

domain_gaps_in_training: Source code / IP theft (no training samples covering code repositories or algorithm exfiltration)
M&A confidential documents (no training samples for merger/acquisition document scenarios)
SAR/AML filings (no training samples for Suspicious Activity Reports or regulatory filings)
Insider trading scenarios (no training samples with pre-earnings or coded-language trading scenarios)
Credentials / API keys (only partial match via KSP-style admin in Slack, not systematically)
Regulatory examination responses (no training samples for exam response documents)
Sanctions evasion (no training samples for shell entity/sanctions scenarios)
Biometric data (no training samples for voice print or biometric data)
Crypto / digital asset data (no training samples for stablecoin, crypto reserve data)
CRM bulk exports (no training samples representing CRM system exports)
ALCO / treasury materials (no training samples for asset-liability committee materials)
Board minutes / governance documents (no training samples for board meeting records)

section_B_axia_gaps_in_training.summary: 32 AXIA benchmark examples have NO matching training data. 23 have only partial/weak document-type coverage but no behavioral context match.

observation_1_alignment.title: Training data is NOT focused on AXIA risk scenarios
observation_1_alignment.detail: AXIA's 55 benchmark examples are insider risk scenarios defined by behavior, intent, destination, and context. The training data is dominated by document-type classification: 'does this document contain PII?' or 'what type of financial document is this?'. Of 30,495 total training samples, 27,953 (91.7%) are from Enron emails and Slack messages classified as 'unknown' topic - they are generic operational communications, not IRM scenarios. The 18 named training topics are document categories (Wire Transfer, Payroll Records, etc.) - these test content sensitivity, not behavioral risk.
observation_1_alignment.key_mismatch: AXIA evaluates WHY data is moving and WHERE it is going. Training data evaluates WHAT the data contains.

observation_2_biggest_gap.title: Behavioral signals are entirely absent from training data
observation_2_biggest_gap.detail: AXIA's severity model is driven by behavioral signals: departure timing, off-hours access, DLP bypass attempts, bulk export volume, destination (dark web, competitor, personal email, social media), and concealment methods. None of these behavioral signals appear in the training data as labeled features. The training data has no samples for: departing employee scenarios, encrypted archive bypasses, API automation abuse, coded language for insider trading, credentials on dark web, or any destination-based risk signals. This is the single largest gap: AXIA's entire differentiation is 'contextual engagement' and 'judgment layer' - but the training data only teaches document content classification.
missing_signal_types: Destination risk (dark web, competitor domain, personal cloud, social media)

observation_3_generic_vs_specific.title: Training data is document-generic; AXIA examples are scenario-specific
observation_3_generic_vs_specific.detail: The training data uses broad document categories like 'Employee Payroll and Salary Records' or 'Wire Transfer or Payment with Account Details'. AXIA's benchmarks are specific scenarios: 'departing sales rep exports CRM via API automation at 2AM' or 'trading algorithm source code zipped and sent to VC firm one week before resignation'. The training data would classify both of these as generically 'high sensitivity' based on document type, but AXIA's severity engine needs to distinguish them from identical documents shared for legitimate business purposes. Example: A wire transfer document sent to an authorized payment processor is LOW risk (example 31-type). The same document modified and sent to an offshore account is CRITICAL (example 5). The training data has no mechanism to distinguish these - they are the same document type.
observation_3_generic_vs_specific.example_conflict: Wire Transfer topic: 813 training samples across ALL sensitivity levels (critical to non-sensitive). This spread suggests the model has learned that wire transfers can be any sensitivity, but not WHEN they are each severity.

observation_4_behavioral_signal_coverage.title: Training data does not cover the behavioral signals AXIA's guidelines emphasize
observation_4_behavioral_signal_coverage.detail: AXIA's IRM guidelines explicitly emphasize: departure proximity, off-hours anomalies, DLP bypass, destination risk, concealment, and pattern-of-life deviations. Checking training data against each: (1) Departure proximity - ZERO training samples. (2) Off-hours access - ZERO training samples with temporal metadata. (3) DLP bypass/encryption - ZERO training samples representing bypass events. (4) Destination analysis - ZERO training samples with recipient risk scoring. (5) Bulk export volume - ZERO training samples with volume/frequency signals. (6) Concealment/obfuscation - ZERO training samples with coded language or steganographic content. The Enron email corpus (48% of samples) captures some corporate communications context, but it was labeled by humans as sensitivity levels for content reasons, not behavioral IRM reasons.
observation_4_behavioral_signal_coverage.recommendation: Training data needs synthetic scenario generation that embeds behavioral context: recipient profiles, timing metadata, volume signals, and departure/access pattern context alongside document content.

observation_5_severity_calibration_risk.title: Severity calibration is misaligned between training data and AXIA benchmark
observation_5_severity_calibration_risk.detail: Critical training data (3,295 samples) is dominated by PII-containing documents: payroll records, loan applications, account opening forms, employee benefits, wire transfers. These are DOCUMENTS with high PII density. AXIA's CRITICAL examples are defined by INTENT + DESTINATION + BEHAVIORAL CONTEXT: encrypted exfiltration to competitors, insider trading, wire fraud, SAR data abuse. The training data would likely produce a model that flags any payroll document as critical regardless of whether it is an authorized HR process or a malicious exfiltration. Conversely, scenarios like example 52 (insider trading using coded language) have NO PII, would likely be labeled 'low sensitivity' by training data patterns, yet are CRITICAL in AXIA's benchmark. This is a fundamental calibration misalignment.
misalignment_examples: Example 52 (coded insider trading language) = likely LOW in training data vs CRITICAL in AXIA
Example 6 (trading algorithm code) = likely LOW/MEDIUM vs CRITICAL in AXIA
Example 21 (product roadmap in Teams) = likely LOW vs HIGH in AXIA
Example 49 (database migration on public drive) = likely HIGH vs CRITICAL in AXIA

observation_6_unknown_topic_quality.title: 65% of training data is unclassified and of uncertain IRM value
observation_6_unknown_topic_quality.detail: 27,953 of 30,495 training samples (91.7%) have topic 'unknown'. These come from Enron emails (73% of unknowns) and Slack (27% of unknowns). Enron emails are real corporate communications but were classified for sensitivity by human annotators - they represent generic business communication, not IRM-specific scenarios. Slack messages are engineering/technical communications (kubectl commands, Terraform configs, OAuth debugging) - valuable for detecting technical credential exposure but not for financial services IRM scenarios. The quality of behavioral signal in these 27,953 samples is unknown without content review. Based on the sample review, most appear to be general business communications that happen to contain some sensitive elements, not deliberate IRM scenario construction.
observation_6_unknown_topic_quality.unknown_percentage: 91.7% of total training data is unclassified by topic
observation_6_unknown_topic_quality.enron_coverage: Enron emails may capture some historical financial fraud context (Enron was an accounting fraud case) but are from 2001 and predate modern DLP/IRM threat models
observation_6_unknown_topic_quality.slack_coverage: Slack samples appear to be engineering communications - relevant for technical IRM but not for financial services fraud scenarios

top_recommendations: Generate synthetic training scenarios for the 30+ AXIA benchmark domains with zero training coverage (M&A docs, SAR data, trading algorithms, credentials, sanctions evasion, crypto)
Add behavioral metadata to training samples: recipient type, timing (off-hours flag), volume (bulk export flag), departure proximity flag
Create balanced training sets where the SAME document type appears across scenarios with different behavioral contexts (authorized vs. malicious) to teach intent discrimination
Specifically construct training scenarios for AXIA's critical behavioral signals: DLP bypass, departure correlation, encrypted exfiltration
Add destination risk as a training signal: personal email vs. corporate email vs. competitor domain vs. dark web vs. social media
Recalibrate severity labeling: documents with PII should not automatically be CRITICAL if shared in authorized contexts; coded-language trading communications should be CRITICAL even without PII
